title: Paragraph and inline meaning

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Paragraph and inline meaning; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: HTML Living Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Paragraph and inline meaning
sources.claim: Paragraph, emphasis, strong importance, quotation, code, time, and related text elements communicate meaning beyond visual styling.

sources.claim: Paragraph, emphasis, strong importance, quotation, code, time, and related text elements communicate meaning beyond visual styling.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Paragraph, emphasis, strong importance, quotation, code, time, and related text elements communicate meaning beyond visual styling.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A warning uses strong for importance while a variable name uses code, allowing meaning to survive without CSS. Your goal is to mark up prose so meaning remains clear without CSS. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Standards-mode document skeleton; Heading hierarchy. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Paragraph, emphasis, strong importance, quotation, code, time, and related text elements communicate meaning beyond visual styling. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Element semantics do not prescribe one appearance, and em or strong should not be used merely to obtain italics or bold. This draft therefore rejects: A span with bold CSS is semantically identical to strong in every context.

includes: Choose semantic text elements for emphasis, importance, quotations, code, time and abbreviations.
Mark up prose so meaning remains clear without CSS.
excludes: Element semantics do not prescribe one appearance, and em or strong should not be used merely to obtain italics or bold.

outcomes: Mark up prose so meaning remains clear without CSS.
